// Auto-generated console guide from playbook. Reviewed and cleaned 2026-04-16.
// Source: lab-01-deploy-the-app.adoc

=== Log in to the OpenShift web console and view available projects

. Click the *Console* tab on the right side of this lab interface.
. Log in with the credentials shown on this page (*Username* and *Password*).
+
TIP: If you see a *Welcome* tour popup, click *Skip tour*.
. After logging in, click the perspective switcher in the top-left corner — it may default to *Developer*. Select *Core platform*.
+
image::console-guide/01-core-platform-projects.png[Core Platform perspective showing all student projects]
+
You will see 5 Projects. Continue to the next section.

=== View all pods running in the datasphere project

. In the Project selector at the top, switch to *`{user}-datasphere`*.
. In the left navigation, expand *Workloads* and click *Pods*.
+
image::console-guide/lab-01-deploy-the-app/02-pods-list-all-running.png[Pods list showing all 3 DataSphere pods running]
+
You will see 3 pods: `datasphere-api`, `datasphere-redis`, and `datasphere-ui`. All show *Running* status with *1/1* Ready and *0* Restarts.

=== Inspect the datasphere-ui pod details including labels and status

. Click the name of the `datasphere-ui-*` pod to open its detail page.
+
image::console-guide/lab-01-deploy-the-app/03-ui-pod-details.png[Pod detail page for datasphere-ui showing Running status, labels (app=datasphere, component=ui), namespace, and pod IP]
+
Notice the *Labels* section: `app=datasphere` and `component=ui`. These labels are how the Service finds this pod. You can also see the *Status* (Running), *Namespace*, *Pod IP*, and other details.
+
Click the *Logs* tab to see the application logs (equivalent to `oc logs`).

=== Inspect the datasphere-ui Service details including selector and endpoints

. In the left navigation, expand *Networking* and click *Services*.
+
image::console-guide/lab-01-deploy-the-app/04-services-list.png[Services list showing datasphere-api, datasphere-ui, and redis with their labels, pod selectors, and cluster IPs]
+
You will see 3 Services. Notice the *Pod selector* column: each Service uses labels to find the right pods.
. Click *datasphere-ui* to open the Service detail page.
+
image::console-guide/lab-01-deploy-the-app/05-service-ui-details.png[Service detail page for datasphere-ui showing pod selector, cluster IP, hostname, and port mapping]
+
Notice the *Pod selector* field: `app=datasphere, component=ui`. This is how the Service finds the right pods. The *Hostname* shows the internal DNS name other applications use to reach this Service by name — no IP addresses required.

=== View the Route details and understand why only the UI has a Route

. In the left navigation, click *Routes*.
+
image::console-guide/lab-01-deploy-the-app/06-routes-list.png[Routes list showing only the datasphere-ui route with Accepted status and its public URL]
+
Notice there is only *one* Route: `datasphere-ui`. The API and Redis have Services for internal communication, but only the frontend needs a Route for external access.
. Click *datasphere-ui* to open the Route detail page.
+
image::console-guide/lab-01-deploy-the-app/07-route-ui-details.png[Route detail page for datasphere-ui showing public URL, host, status Accepted, service, and target port 8080]
+
The *Location* field is the public URL you opened in your browser. OpenShift generated the hostname automatically and handles the TLS certificate — there is nothing to configure.

=== Deploy datasphere-ui from a container image using the web console

. Switch to the *Developer* perspective using the perspective switcher in the top-left.
. Confirm the *`{user}-lab`* project is selected in the Project selector.
. In the left navigation, click *+Add*, then click *Container images*.
+
image::console-guide/lab-01-deploy-the-app/09-deploy-image-form.png[Deploy Image form with datasphere-ui image name entered from external registry]
+
. In the *Image name from external registry* field, enter: `{datasphere_ui_image}`
. Set *Name* to `datasphere-ui`.
. Scroll down to *Advanced options*. Confirm *Target port* is *8080* and check the *Create a route* checkbox — this tells OpenShift to create a public HTTPS Route automatically.
+
image::console-guide/lab-01-deploy-the-app/11-deploy-image-create-route-checked.png[Deploy Image form Advanced Options with Create a route checkbox checked and target port 8080]
+
. Click *Create*. OpenShift creates a Deployment, a Service, and an edge-terminated Route in one step — the console equivalent of `oc new-app` followed by `oc create route edge`.

=== Build and deploy the API from Git source using Source-to-Image

. In the left navigation, click *+Add*, then click *Import from Git*.
+
image::console-guide/lab-01-deploy-the-app/10-import-from-git-form.png[Import from Git form with Gitea datasphere-api repository URL and advanced Git options showing Source Secret field for authentication]
+
. In the *Git Repo URL* field, enter your Gitea repository URL: `{gitea_url}/{user}/datasphere-api`
+
NOTE: If you see a warning about the repository being unreachable, click *Show advanced Git options* and select a *Source Secret* with your Gitea credentials. The CLI command handles private repositories automatically.
+
. OpenShift detects the Python language and selects the appropriate builder image automatically.
. Set *Name* to `datasphere-api`, then click *Create*.

=== Monitor the S2I build progress and confirm both components are running

After clicking *Create*, OpenShift schedules an S2I build. To watch it:

. Click *Builds* in the left navigation.
. Click the *datasphere-api* BuildConfig that appears.
. Select the *Builds* tab — a new Build entry shows the current run.
. Click the build name and select *Logs* to see the live build output: cloning the repository, installing Python dependencies, building and pushing the image.

When the build status shows *Complete*, the Deployment starts automatically. Navigate to *Topology* to confirm both `datasphere-ui` and `datasphere-api` appear with solid blue rings.

image::console-guide/lab-01-deploy-the-app/12-topology-s2i-api-deployed.png[Topology view showing datasphere-ui and datasphere-api both deployed with the external Route icon on datasphere-ui]

The external link icon on `datasphere-ui` is the Route. Click it to open the app — the map is now populated with live data from the API you just deployed.

=== Verify the Helm release in the console after running helm install from the terminal

. Switch to the *`{user}-lab-helm`* Project and click *Helm* in the left navigation.
+
image::console-guide/lab-01-deploy-the-app/13-helm-releases-empty.png[Helm Releases page showing No Helm Releases found — starting state before helm install]
+
NOTE: The DataSphere chart is served from your Gitea package registry as a direct chart URL. The console's Helm catalog only shows pre-registered chart repositories — use the terminal to run `helm install` as shown in the CLI steps.
+
. After the `helm install` command completes in the terminal, return to this *Helm* page. A *datasphere* release appears showing *Status: deployed* and *Revision: 1*. Each `helm upgrade` increments the revision — Helm tracks the full history here.
. Switch to *Topology* to see all three components come up together from a single install.
+
image::console-guide/lab-01-deploy-the-app/12-topology-s2i-api-deployed.png[Topology view showing all three DataSphere components — datasphere-ui, datasphere-api, and datasphere-redis — deployed by the Helm chart]
+
The external link icon on `datasphere-ui` is the Route created by the chart. Click it to open the application.
